ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM covers prepared sea cucumber; no specific stir-fried sea cucumber with mushroom composite code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.

allergens: shellfish-adjacent (sea cucumber — not a crustacean but a marine invertebrate; cross-reactivity possible)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried sea cucumber with mushroom as a side dish in a Singaporean meal is approximately 150 g, equivalent to roughly 3–4 pieces of sea cucumber with mushrooms.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Sea Cucumber with Mushroom — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Stir Fried Sea Cucumber with Mushroom (cr_id F14080055). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable stir-fry with protein (GI ~35)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried sea cucumber with mushroom. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed vegetable and protein stir-fry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 3.7 g/100g, predominantly from vegetables and a small amount of cornstarch thickener. GL per 150 g serving = (35 × 3.7 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.9 g, classified as very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 482 mg/100g — borderline high for a side dish
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–120 g per meal and request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce during preparation to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per serving; estimated potassium at ~220 mg/100g provides partial but insufficient offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (3.7 g/100g), low GI (~35), moderate dietary fibre (2.5 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content, low estimated glycaemic index, and moderate fibre; cornstarch thickening adds a small amount of refined carbohydrate, so portion control and pairing with low-GI staples is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 482 mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low (~0.9 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat content and absence of trans fat are favourable for cardiovascular health; however, the elevated sodium at 482 mg/100g warrants portion restriction to 100–120 g and preparation modifications to reduce added salt, soy sauce, and oyster sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds from mushroom beta-glucans, ginger gingerols, and sea cucumber peptides; low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis given its anti-inflammatory ingredient profile; sodium at 482 mg/100g may worsen tissue swelling, so reduced-seasoning preparation is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Sea Cucumber with Mushroom (海参炒蘑菇) is a Singaporean Chinese dish featuring rehydrated sea cucumber and fresh or dried mushrooms cooked in a savoury black bean sauce with aromatics including garlic, ginger, and spring onion, finished with oyster sauce, soy sauce, and a light cornstarch glaze. Sea cucumber has a gelatinous, slightly chewy texture with a mild, neutral flavour that absorbs the surrounding sauce. Mushrooms contribute an earthy umami depth. The dish is typically served as a side dish in a shared Chinese meal alongside steamed rice. Nutritionally, it provides 89 kcal per 100 g with 5.7 g protein, 5.8 g fat, and only 3.7 g carbohydrate, making it a low-carbohydrate, moderate-protein option. Dietary fibre is 2.5 g/100g. Sodium is elevated at 482 mg/100g due to black bean sauce, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. Sea cucumber is a traditional ingredient in Chinese cuisine prized for its collagen content and documented anti-inflammatory peptides. Mushrooms contribute beta-glucans with immunomodulatory properties. The dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate and low glycaemic index profile, but sodium content requires caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is dairy-free and halal-compatible.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080055) at confidence 0.95. The dish is an exact match in the primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite stir-fried dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry. cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. potassium_mg value of 220 mg/100g is an estimate referenced from the input record's health suitability notes; not directly reported in HPB source. sugar_g value of 1.5 g/100g is an estimate consistent with the input record's health suitability notes; not directly reported in HPB source. saturated_fat_g of 0.9 g/100g is estimated from the input record's health suitability notes (~1 g/100g); not directly reported in HPB source. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed absent per input record clinical notes. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Dietary tags 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' from the input record have been removed as sea cucumber is an animal product. 'keto' tag retained as carbohydrate content (3.7 g/100g) is consistent with ketogenic dietary thresholds. Input description contained a typographical error ('mushroon') which has been corrected to 'mushroom'.
